1:
"Whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God: and every one that loveth him that begat loveth him also that is begotten of him." The witnesses note that faith in Christ marks the new birth, and love of the Father issues in love of his children. Augustine notes the connection with the foregoing, that he who loves God must love his brother also; the begetting love runs from the Father to all who are begotten of him. The witnesses note John declaring that whoever believes Jesus is the Christ is born of God, and everyone who loves the Father loves also him that is begotten of him; the new birth shown by faith, and love of God issuing in love of the brethren — that John joins faith and love as the twin marks of the regenerate: "whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God" (true, saving faith in Jesus as the Christ is the fruit and evidence of the new birth, for none can so believe but the regenerate) — "and every one that loveth him that begat loveth him also that is begotten of him"; the witnesses note the family argument — love to "him that begat" (the Father) necessarily flows out to "him also that is begotten of him" (every child of God, the brethren); so that he who truly loves the Father will love all the Father's children, faith and brotherly love being inseparable in the born-again soul; whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God. The witnesses note whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God. The witnesses note every one that loveth him that begat loveth him also that is begotten of him.

The witnesses note faith in Christ as the mark of the new birth, and love of the Father issuing in love of his children — faith and brotherly love inseparable in the born-again soul.

So the witnesses make the verse faith and love as twin marks of the regenerate. The lesson the expositors draw is that saving faith in Christ evidences the new birth, and love of the Father flows out to all his children. The witnesses note the believeth: "whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God" — saving faith the fruit of the new birth; the believeth; the saving faith; the the fruit; the the new birth; the regenerate. The expositors observe the none but the regenerate: none can so believe but the regenerate; the none but regenerate; the can so believe; the the new birth; the the fruit; the evidence. The witnesses press the loveth him that begat: "every one that loveth him that begat" — love to the Father; the loveth him that begat; the the Father; the love to God; the the begetter; the love. The expositors draw the lesson on the loveth the begotten: "loveth him also that is begotten of him" — love flows to his children; the loveth the begotten; the his children; the the brethren; the flows; the love. The witnesses observe the family argument: Augustine notes the begetting love runs to all the begotten; the family argument; the Augustine; the the begotten; the all; the flows. The expositors note the inseparable: faith and brotherly love are inseparable; the inseparable; the faith; the brotherly love; the the marks; the the regenerate. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God, the every one that loveth him that begat loveth him also that is begotten of him, and faith and love. The verse thus records faith and love as twin marks of the regenerate — teaching that saving faith evidences the new birth, and love of the Father flows out to all his children.
2:
"By this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God, and keep his commandments." The witnesses note the test by which true love of the brethren is known. Gill notes the apostle's view is to show when love to the saints is right, namely, when we love God and keep his commandments. The witnesses note John declaring that by this we know we love the children of God — when we love God and keep his commandments; the test of true brotherly love in the love of God and obedience to him — that John, having said love of God flows to his children (v.1), now turns it about to give the test: "by this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God, and keep his commandments"; the witnesses note the mutual proof — love to God's children is proved genuine when joined to love of God and obedience, and love of God is proved real by love to his children; so that the two loves and obedience all hang together, each testing and confirming the other; true love to the brethren is not a mere natural fondness, but springs from love to God and shows itself in keeping his commandments; by this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God, and keep his commandments. The witnesses note by this we know that we love the children of God. The witnesses note when we love God, and keep his commandments.

The witnesses note the test of true brotherly love — known when we love God and keep his commandments; love to the brethren springing from love to God and shown in obedience.

So the witnesses make the verse the test of true love by love of God and obedience. The lesson the expositors draw is that genuine love to the brethren is proved by love of God and the keeping of his commandments. The witnesses note the by this we know: "by this we know that we love the children of God" — Gill notes the test of right love to the saints; the by this we know; the the test; the right love; the the saints; the proved. The expositors observe the love God: "when we love God" — joined to love of God; the love God; the of God; the joined; the the love; the genuine. The witnesses press the keep his commandments: "and keep his commandments" — and obedience to him; the keep his commandments; the obedience; the the commandments; the obey; the keep. The expositors draw the lesson on the mutual proof: love to the children is proved by love of God, and vice versa; the mutual proof; the each tests; the confirms; the the other; the proved. The witnesses observe the not natural fondness: not a mere natural fondness; the not natural fondness; the not mere; the springs from God; the love; the genuine. The expositors note the all hang together: the two loves and obedience all hang together; the all hang together; the the two loves; the obedience; the each; the confirms. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the by this we know that we love the children of God, the when we love God and keep his commandments, and the test of love. The verse thus records the test of true love by love of God and obedience — teaching that genuine love to the brethren is proved by love of God and the keeping of his commandments.
3:
"For this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments: and his commandments are not grievous." The witnesses note that love of God consists in keeping his commandments, which are not burdensome. Gill notes keeping the commandments is an evidence of love to God, showing that love is not in word and tongue only, but in deed and in truth, and that such have a sense of the love of God upon their souls. The witnesses note John declaring that this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments, and his commandments are not grievous; the proof of love in obedience, and the lightness of God's commands to the believer — that John defines love of God in practical terms: "for this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments" (the true love of God shows itself not in mere profession but in obedience, the keeping of his commandments being the very proof and fruit of love) — and he adds a sweet encouragement: "and his commandments are not grievous"; the witnesses note the comfort — to the believer, made willing and given a new nature, God's commandments are "not grievous" (not heavy, burdensome, or irksome, as they are to the unwilling), but, being kept in love and by the help of grace, are light and even delightful; so that love makes obedience, and obedience love makes easy; his commandments are not grievous. The witnesses note this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments. The witnesses note and his commandments are not grievous.

The witnesses note love of God shown in keeping his commandments, which are not grievous — obedience the proof of love, and God's commands light and delightful to the willing, grace-helped believer.

So the witnesses make the verse love proved by obedience, and the commands not burdensome. The lesson the expositors draw is that love of God consists in keeping his commandments, which to the believer are not grievous but light. The witnesses note the love of God: "this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments" — Gill notes obedience the proof of love; the love of God; the keep his commandments; the obedience; the the proof; the love. The expositors observe the not in word only: love is not in word only but in deed; the not in word only; the not profession; the in deed; the in truth; the obedience. The witnesses press the not grievous: "and his commandments are not grievous" — not heavy or burdensome; the not grievous; the not heavy; the not burdensome; the not irksome; the light. The expositors draw the lesson on the to the willing: not grievous to the willing, made so by grace; the to the willing; the made willing; the new nature; the grace; the light. The witnesses observe the light and delightful: kept in love, they are light and delightful; the light and delightful; the in love; the by grace; the easy; the delight. The expositors note the love makes easy: love makes obedience, and obedience love makes easy; the love makes easy; the obedience; the love; the easy; the delight. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this is the love of God that we keep his commandments, the and his commandments are not grievous, and love proved by obedience. The verse thus records love proved by obedience and the commands not burdensome — teaching that love of God consists in keeping his commandments, which to the believer are light.
4:
"For whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world: and this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith." The witnesses note that the new birth gives victory over the world through faith. Gill notes "whatsoever is born of God" may be understood of persons born of God, or of the new principle of grace wrought in them, particularly faith — a heaven-born grace, the gift of God and the operation of his Spirit. The witnesses note John declaring that whatsoever is born of God overcomes the world, and this is the victory that overcomes the world, even our faith; the victory over the world won by the faith of the new-born soul — that John gives the reason why God's commands are not grievous: the regenerate has a conquering power: "for whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world" (every one born of God, and the new principle of grace in him, gains the victory over the world — its frowns and flatteries, its sins and snares) — and he names the weapon of this conquest: "and this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith"; the witnesses note that it is faith, the heaven-born grace, that overcomes — faith laying hold of Christ and the unseen world, by which the believer rises above and triumphs over the world that would master others; so that the new birth makes overcomers, and faith is the victory; this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith. The witnesses note for whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world. The witnesses note this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith.

The witnesses note the victory over the world won by the new-born soul through faith — faith laying hold of Christ, by which the believer rises above and triumphs over the world.

So the witnesses make the verse the victory over the world by faith. The lesson the expositors draw is that the regenerate overcomes the world, and faith is the victory that does it. The witnesses note the born of God overcometh: "whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world" — Gill notes the regenerate gains the victory; the born of God overcometh; the the regenerate; the the victory; the the world; the conquers. The expositors observe the the world: its frowns, flatteries, sins, and snares; the the world; the the frowns; the the flatteries; the the sins; the the snares. The witnesses press the the victory: "this is the victory that overcometh the world" — the conquest; the the victory; the overcometh; the the conquest; the triumph; the the world. The expositors draw the lesson on the our faith: "even our faith" — faith is the weapon and victory; the our faith; the the weapon; the the victory; the faith; the the grace. The witnesses observe the laying hold of Christ: faith laying hold of Christ and the unseen world; the laying hold of Christ; the the unseen; the Christ; the the world; the rises above. The expositors note the makes overcomers: the new birth makes overcomers; the makes overcomers; the the new birth; the overcomers; the faith; the victory. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world, the this is the victory that overcometh the world even our faith, and the victory by faith. The verse thus records the victory over the world by faith — teaching that the regenerate overcomes the world, and faith is the victory that does it.
5:
"Who is he that overcometh the world, but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God?" The witnesses note that only the believer in Christ overcomes the world. Gill notes this question carries a strong affirmation, that no other person is the conqueror of the world. Barnes notes all else are worldly and governed by the world, and only he who believes in the Saviour overcomes it. The witnesses note John asking who overcomes the world but he that believes Jesus is the Son of God; the overcomer identified as the believer in Christ the Son of God — that John, by a pointed question, fixes the identity of the world-conqueror: "who is he that overcometh the world, but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God?"; the witnesses note the force of the question — it is a strong way of affirming that there is no other overcomer at all; none but the one who truly believes that Jesus is "the Son of God" gains the victory; all others, however strong or wise, are mastered by the world and live under its power; but the believer, his faith fixed on the divine Son, rises above it and conquers; so that the world-overcoming faith is faith in the deity and Sonship of Jesus; he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God. The witnesses note who is he that overcometh the world. The witnesses note but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God.

The witnesses note the overcomer as the believer in Christ — none overcomes the world but he who believes Jesus is the Son of God; all others mastered by it, the believer rising above and conquering.

So the witnesses make the verse the overcomer identified as the believer. The lesson the expositors draw is that none overcomes the world but the one who believes Jesus is the Son of God. The witnesses note the question: "who is he that overcometh the world" — Gill notes a strong affirmation; the question; the strong affirmation; the no other; the the overcomer; the the conqueror. The expositors observe the all else worldly: Barnes notes all else are worldly, governed by the world; the all else worldly; the governed; the the world; the mastered; the under it. The witnesses press the believeth Jesus: "but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God" — the believer in Christ the Son; the believeth Jesus; the the believer; the the Son of God; the faith; the fixed. The expositors draw the lesson on the no other: none but the believer overcomes; the no other; the none but; the the believer; the overcomes; the the victory. The witnesses observe the rises above: the believer rises above and conquers; the rises above; the conquers; the the believer; the the world; the triumphs. The expositors note the faith in his Sonship: the world-overcoming faith is faith in the Son of God; the faith in his Sonship; the the deity; the the Son; the faith; the overcomes. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who is he that overcometh the world, the but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God, and the overcomer the believer. The verse thus records the overcomer identified as the believer — teaching that none overcomes the world but the one who believes Jesus is the Son of God.
6:
"This is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ; not by water only, but by water and blood. And it is the Spirit that beareth witness, because the Spirit is truth." The witnesses note Christ coming by water and blood, witnessed by the Spirit. Gill notes by "water" is not meant the ceremonial washings, nor only the purity of his nature and life, but rather his baptism and the cleansing of the Spirit; and by "blood," his atoning death. The witnesses note John declaring that this is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ, not by water only but by water and blood, and the Spirit bears witness because the Spirit is truth; Christ's coming by water and blood and the Spirit's witness to it — that John sets out the witnesses to Christ's true Sonship and saving work: "this is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ; not by water only, but by water and blood"; the witnesses note the debate on the meaning of "water and blood" — some take them of Christ's baptism (water) and his atoning death (blood); others of the two streams, water and blood, that flowed from his pierced side; others of the cleansing and the atoning work of Christ, or the two sacraments — but all agree that Christ came not by one only but by both, both his cleansing and his atoning being needful — "and it is the Spirit that beareth witness, because the Spirit is truth" (the Holy Spirit adds his own sure testimony, being himself the truth); so that Christ's saving work is confirmed by the witness of the Spirit; this is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ. The witnesses note this is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ; not by water only, but by water and blood. The witnesses note it is the Spirit that beareth witness, because the Spirit is truth.

The witnesses note Christ coming by water and blood — debated as his baptism and death, or the streams from his side, or his cleansing and atoning — witnessed by the Spirit, who is truth.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's coming by water and blood, witnessed by the Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ came by both water and blood, his coming confirmed by the witness of the Spirit of truth. The witnesses note the came by water and blood: "this is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ" — Christ's coming attested by water and blood; the came by water and blood; the Christ; the attested; the water; the blood. The witnesses present the debate: the witnesses note the debate on "water and blood" — some of his baptism and atoning death, others of the streams from his pierced side, others of his cleansing and atoning work or the sacraments. The expositors observe the not water only: "not by water only, but by water and blood" — not one only but both; the not water only; the both; the by water and blood; the needful; the cleansing and atoning. The expositors draw the lesson on the the Spirit beareth witness: "it is the Spirit that beareth witness" — the Spirit adds his testimony; the the Spirit beareth witness; the the Spirit; the testimony; the adds; the witness. The witnesses observe the the Spirit is truth: "because the Spirit is truth" — being himself the truth; the the Spirit is truth; the himself; the the truth; the witness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it is the Spirit that beareth witness because the Spirit is truth, and Christ's coming. The verse thus records Christ's coming by water and blood, witnessed by the Spirit — teaching that Christ came by both, confirmed by the Spirit of truth.
7:
"For there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost: and these three are one." The witnesses note the heavenly witness of the Trinity to Christ's Sonship. Gill notes the genuineness of this text has been called in question by some, since it is wanting in the Syriac, Arabic, and Ethiopic versions, and the old Latin interpreter; yet it is found in many Greek copies and ancient writers, and the sense agrees with the doctrine of the Trinity. The witnesses note John declaring that there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost, and these three are one; the threefold heavenly witness of the Trinity, with debate over the verse's genuineness — that John (in this much-discussed verse) sets forth the highest witness to the Sonship of Christ: "for there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost"; the witnesses note the great textual debate — the genuineness of this clause (the "Johannine Comma") is disputed, for it is absent from the Syriac, Arabic, Ethiopic, and old Latin, and many Greek manuscripts, though found in others and cited by some ancients; yet the witnesses note that the doctrine it teaches is firmly grounded elsewhere in Scripture — the three Persons of the Godhead, "the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost," bear united witness, and "these three are one" (one in nature, essence, and testimony), a clear statement of the unity of the Trinity; these three are one. The witnesses note there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost. The witnesses note and these three are one.

The witnesses note the threefold heavenly witness — the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost, three that are one — with the textual debate over the genuineness of this disputed clause.

So the witnesses make the verse the heavenly witness of the Trinity. The lesson the expositors draw is that the three divine Persons bear united witness and are one, though the verse's genuineness is debated. The witnesses note the three in heaven: "there are three that bear record in heaven" — the heavenly witnesses; the three in heaven; the the witnesses; the bear record; the heaven; the threefold. The witnesses present the textual debate: the witnesses note the genuineness is disputed — absent from the Syriac, Arabic, Ethiopic, old Latin, and many Greek copies, though found in others and cited by some ancients. The expositors observe the the Father the Word: "the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost" — the three divine Persons; the the Father; the the Word; the the Holy Ghost; the the Persons; the the Godhead. The witnesses press the bear witness: they bear united witness to Christ's Sonship; the bear witness; the united; the Christ's Sonship; the the record; the testimony. The expositors draw the lesson on the these three are one: "and these three are one" — one in nature and testimony; the these three are one; the one; the in nature; the in essence; the the Trinity. The witnesses observe the doctrine grounded: the doctrine is firmly grounded elsewhere in Scripture; the doctrine grounded; the firmly; the elsewhere; the Scripture; the the Trinity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the there are three that bear record in heaven the Father the Word and the Holy Ghost, the and these three are one, and the heavenly witness. The verse thus records the heavenly witness of the Trinity — teaching that the three divine Persons bear united witness and are one, though the verse's genuineness is debated.
8:
"And there are three that bear witness in earth, the Spirit, and the water, and the blood: and these three agree in one." The witnesses note the threefold earthly witness to Christ's Sonship. Gill notes these three bear witness on earth to the same truth of the sonship of Christ; Barnes notes that the genuine passage continues the testimony of the Spirit, the water, and the blood. The witnesses note John declaring that there are three that bear witness on earth — the Spirit, the water, and the blood — and these three agree in one; the threefold earthly witness uniting in testimony to Christ — that John names a second set of witnesses, on earth, to the truth of Christ's Sonship and saving work: "there are three that bear witness in earth, the Spirit, and the water, and the blood: and these three agree in one"; the witnesses note the harmony — the "Spirit" (the Holy Spirit's testimony in the word and in the hearts of believers), the "water" (Christ's baptism and cleansing), and the "blood" (his atoning death) all bear one consistent witness; and "these three agree in one" — they concur in a single testimony, that Jesus is the Son of God and the Saviour; so that earth as well as heaven has its threefold witness, all uniting to confirm the believer's faith in Christ; these three agree in one. The witnesses note there are three that bear witness in earth, the Spirit, and the water, and the blood. The witnesses note and these three agree in one.

The witnesses note the threefold earthly witness — the Spirit, the water, and the blood — uniting in one testimony that Jesus is the Son of God; earth as well as heaven bearing witness to Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the earthly witness uniting to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Spirit, the water, and the blood agree in one testimony to Christ's Sonship and saving work. The witnesses note the three in earth: "there are three that bear witness in earth" — Gill notes the earthly witnesses to Christ's Sonship; the three in earth; the the earthly witnesses; the Christ's Sonship; the bear witness; the threefold. The expositors observe the the Spirit: "the Spirit" — the Holy Spirit's testimony; the the Spirit; the the Holy Spirit; the the word; the the hearts; the testimony. The witnesses press the the water: "and the water" — Christ's baptism and cleansing; the the water; the baptism; the cleansing; the Christ; the the witness. The expositors draw the lesson on the the blood: "and the blood" — his atoning death; the the blood; the atoning; the death; the the witness; the cleanses. The witnesses observe the agree in one: "and these three agree in one" — they concur in one testimony; the agree in one; the concur; the one testimony; the consistent; the united. The expositors note the earth has its witness: earth as well as heaven has its threefold witness; the earth has its witness; the earth; the heaven; the threefold; the confirm. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the there are three that bear witness in earth the Spirit and the water and the blood, the and these three agree in one, and the earthly witness. The verse thus records the earthly witness uniting to Christ — teaching that the Spirit, the water, and the blood agree in one testimony to Christ's Sonship.
9:
"If we receive the witness of men, the witness of God is greater: for this is the witness of God which he hath testified of his Son." The witnesses note that God's witness to his Son is greater than any human testimony. Gill notes the witness of a sufficient number of credible men, of good character, is always admitted in any court; on this men are justified or condemned, and so much more is the witness of God to be received. The witnesses note John reasoning that, if we receive the witness of men, the witness of God is greater, this being the witness God testified of his Son; the surpassing weight of God's own witness to his Son — that John draws the argument from the lesser to the greater: "if we receive the witness of men" (as we daily do, accepting the testimony of credible witnesses in every matter of life and law) "the witness of God is greater" — how much more should we receive the testimony of God himself, which is of infinitely greater weight and certainty — "for this is the witness of God which he hath testified of his Son"; the witnesses note the point — the witnesses just named (Spirit, water, blood, and the heavenly Three) are God's own testimony to his Son; and if men readily believe one another, it is the height of unbelief and folly to refuse the witness of the God who cannot lie, given concerning his Son; the witness of God is greater. The witnesses note if we receive the witness of men, the witness of God is greater. The witnesses note for this is the witness of God which he hath testified of his Son.

The witnesses note God's witness to his Son surpassing all human testimony — if we receive men's witness, how much more God's, of infinitely greater weight; to refuse it the height of unbelief.

So the witnesses make the verse the surpassing weight of God's witness. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's own testimony to his Son far surpasses men's, so to refuse it is the height of unbelief. The witnesses note the witness of men: "if we receive the witness of men" — Gill notes we daily accept credible men's testimony; the witness of men; the credible men; the daily; the accept; the testimony. The expositors observe the in court: on men's witness men are justified or condemned; the in court; the justified; the condemned; the law; the testimony. The witnesses press the witness of God greater: "the witness of God is greater" — God's testimony of infinitely greater weight; the witness of God greater; the greater; the infinitely; the the weight; the certainty. The expositors draw the lesson on the testified of his Son: "this is the witness of God which he hath testified of his Son" — God's testimony to his Son; the testified of his Son; the God's testimony; the his Son; the the witness; the given. The witnesses observe the the witnesses are God's: the witnesses named are God's own testimony; the the witnesses are God's; the the Spirit; the water; the blood; the testimony. The expositors note the height of unbelief: to refuse God's witness is the height of unbelief; the height of unbelief; the refuse; the God's witness; the folly; the cannot lie. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if we receive the witness of men the witness of God is greater, the for this is the witness of God which he hath testified of his Son, and God's surpassing witness. The verse thus records the surpassing weight of God's witness — teaching that God's testimony to his Son far surpasses men's, so to refuse it is the height of unbelief.
10:
"He that believeth on the Son of God hath the witness in himself: he that believeth not God hath made him a liar; because he believeth not the record that God gave of his Son." The witnesses note the inward witness of the believer and the guilt of unbelief. Gill notes he that believes on the Son of God — as a divine person, who obeyed the law and brought in everlasting righteousness — has him as the object of his faith, and has the witness in himself. The witnesses note John declaring that he that believes on the Son of God has the witness in himself, but he that believes not has made God a liar, not believing the record of his Son; the inward witness of faith and the great guilt of refusing God's testimony — that John shows the two opposite states: "he that believeth on the Son of God hath the witness in himself" (the believer has an inward, experimental witness — the Spirit's testimony in his heart, the assurance and evidence of the truth he believes, written within him) — while "he that believeth not God hath made him a liar; because he believeth not the record that God gave of his Son"; the witnesses note the dreadful charge against unbelief — to refuse God's testimony concerning his Son is, in effect, to call God a liar, charging the God of truth with falsehood; so that the believer carries the witness within, but the unbeliever, by his very unbelief, commits the awful sin of giving God the lie; he that believeth on the Son of God hath the witness in himself. The witnesses note he that believeth on the Son of God hath the witness in himself. The witnesses note he that believeth not God hath made him a liar.

The witnesses note the believer's inward witness and the guilt of unbelief — the believer has the witness in himself, while the unbeliever, refusing God's testimony, makes God a liar.

So the witnesses make the verse the inward witness and the guilt of unbelief. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer has the witness within, while the unbeliever, refusing God's testimony, charges God with falsehood. The witnesses note the witness in himself: "he that believeth on the Son of God hath the witness in himself" — Gill notes an inward, experimental witness; the witness in himself; the inward; the experimental; the the Spirit; the assurance. The expositors observe the the Spirit's testimony: the Spirit's testimony in the heart; the the Spirit's testimony; the the heart; the assurance; the evidence; the within. The witnesses press the believeth not: "he that believeth not God" — the unbeliever refusing God's witness; the believeth not; the refusing; the God's witness; the the unbeliever; the rejects. The expositors draw the lesson on the made him a liar: "hath made him a liar" — calls God a liar; the made him a liar; the calls God; the a liar; the falsehood; the the charge. The witnesses observe the the record: "because he believeth not the record that God gave of his Son" — refusing the record of his Son; the the record; the of his Son; the refusing; the God gave; the rejects. The expositors note the gives God the lie: by unbelief he gives the God of truth the lie; the gives God the lie; the the God of truth; the falsehood; the unbelief; the the sin. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that believeth on the Son of God hath the witness in himself, the he that believeth not God hath made him a liar, and the inward witness. The verse thus records the inward witness and the guilt of unbelief — teaching that the believer has the witness within, while the unbeliever makes God a liar.
11:
"And this is the record, that God hath given to us eternal life, and this life is in his Son." The witnesses note the sum of God's record — eternal life given in his Son. Barnes notes this is the sum, or the amount, of the testimony which God has given respecting his Son. Gill notes the sum and substance of the record concerns the person of Christ and the security of salvation in him, who is the true God and eternal life. The witnesses note John declaring that this is the record — that God has given us eternal life, and this life is in his Son; the substance of God's testimony — eternal life freely given, and treasured in Christ — that John gives the very heart and content of God's witness: "and this is the record, that God hath given to us eternal life, and this life is in his Son"; the witnesses note the two great truths — first, "God hath given to us eternal life" (eternal life is God's free gift, not earned, but bestowed on his people in his love) — and second, "this life is in his Son" (the life is treasured up and to be found in Christ alone, not apart from him); so that the whole testimony of God comes to this gracious sum: eternal life is the gift of God, and it is laid up in his Son, so that to have the Son is to have life; this is the record, that God hath given to us eternal life. The witnesses note this is the record, that God hath given to us eternal life. The witnesses note and this life is in his Son.

The witnesses note the substance of God's record — eternal life freely given, and treasured in his Son; eternal life God's gift, laid up in Christ, so that to have the Son is to have life.

So the witnesses make the verse the sum of God's record — life given in his Son. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's testimony comes to this: eternal life is God's free gift, treasured in Christ. The witnesses note the the record: "this is the record" — Barnes notes the sum of God's testimony; the the record; the the sum; the the testimony; the God's witness; the the substance. The expositors observe the given eternal life: "that God hath given to us eternal life" — eternal life is God's free gift; the given eternal life; the free gift; the not earned; the bestowed; the his love. The witnesses press the in his Son: "and this life is in his Son" — the life is in Christ alone; the in his Son; the in Christ; the alone; the treasured; the laid up. The expositors draw the lesson on the two truths: the two great truths — life given, life in the Son; the two truths; the life given; the life in the Son; the the gift; the in Christ. The witnesses observe the to have the Son: to have the Son is to have life; the to have the Son; the to have life; the in Christ; the the gift; the laid up. The expositors note the gracious sum: God's whole testimony comes to this gracious sum; the gracious sum; the the testimony; the comes to; the the gift; the in his Son. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this is the record that God hath given to us eternal life, the and this life is in his Son, and the sum of God's record. The verse thus records the sum of God's record — teaching that eternal life is God's free gift, treasured in Christ.
12:
"He that hath the Son hath life; and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life." The witnesses note that life is bound up with having the Son. Gill notes he that hath the Son has a spiritual and experimental knowledge of him, true faith in him, and has him dwelling in his heart and living in him. Barnes notes John refers to the principle laid down by the Saviour, that he that has the Son has life. The witnesses note John declaring that he that hath the Son hath life, and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life; the having of the Son the dividing line between life and death — that John draws the sharp, dividing conclusion from the record (v.11): since the life is in the Son, it follows plainly that "he that hath the Son hath life" (the one who has Christ — by faith possessing, knowing, and being united to him — has eternal life, for the life is in Christ and so in all who have him) — "and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life"; the witnesses note the absoluteness of the statement — there is no middle ground: to have the Son is to have life; to be without the Son is to be without life, however else a man may seem to live; so that everything turns on the one question, whether a man has the Son of God; he that hath the Son hath life. The witnesses note he that hath the Son hath life. The witnesses note he that hath not the Son of God hath not life.

The witnesses note the having of the Son as the dividing line between life and death — to have Christ is to have life, to be without him is to be without life; everything turning on this one question.

So the witnesses make the verse the having of the Son as life or death. The lesson the expositors draw is that to have the Son is to have life, and to be without him is to be without life, with no middle ground. The witnesses note the hath the Son: "he that hath the Son hath life" — Gill notes having Christ by faith and union; the hath the Son; the by faith; the union; the possessing; the Christ. The expositors observe the dwelling in him: Christ dwelling in the heart, the believer living in him; the dwelling in him; the the heart; the living in him; the united; the faith. The witnesses press the hath life: "hath life" — has eternal life, for the life is in Christ; the hath life; the eternal life; the in Christ; the the life; the possesses. The expositors draw the lesson on the hath not the Son: "he that hath not the Son of God" — the one without Christ; the hath not the Son; the without Christ; the the one; the lacks; the not united. The witnesses observe the hath not life: "hath not life" — is without life; the hath not life; the without life; the no life; the dead; the lacks. The expositors note the no middle ground: there is no middle ground between life and death; the no middle ground; the life or death; the the question; the the Son; the turns. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that hath the Son hath life, the and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life, and the dividing line. The verse thus records the having of the Son as life or death — teaching that to have the Son is to have life, and to be without him is to be without life.
13:
"These things have I written unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God; that ye may know that ye have eternal life, and that ye may believe on the name of the Son of God." The witnesses note the purpose of the epistle — that believers may know they have eternal life. Gill notes "these things have I written unto you" — what is contained in the epistle in general, and particularly concerning the victory over the world and the witnesses of Christ's sonship and the record God bore. The witnesses note John declaring that he wrote these things to believers that they may know they have eternal life and believe on the name of the Son of God; the assuring aim of the letter — that believers may know they have eternal life — that John states the great purpose of his writing: "these things have I written unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God; that ye may know that ye have eternal life"; the witnesses note the gracious end — John writes not to the unbelieving but "to you that believe," and his aim is their assurance: "that ye may know that ye have eternal life" — that believers might have, not a doubtful hope, but a settled knowledge and assurance of their present possession of eternal life — "and that ye may believe on the name of the Son of God" (and so be confirmed and grow in their faith); so that the whole epistle is given that believers may be sure they have life and may believe the more firmly; that ye may know that ye have eternal life. The witnesses note these things have I written unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God. The witnesses note that ye may know that ye have eternal life.

The witnesses note the assuring aim of the letter — that believers may know they have eternal life; John writing not to the doubting but that the faithful might have settled assurance of their present possession.

So the witnesses make the verse the assuring purpose of the epistle. The lesson the expositors draw is that John writes to believers that they may know with settled assurance that they have eternal life. The witnesses note the written to believers: "these things have I written unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God" — Gill notes written to believers; the written to believers; the the faithful; the believe; the the Son of God; the to you. The expositors observe the these things: what is contained in the epistle, the victory and witnesses; the these things; the the epistle; the the victory; the the witnesses; the the record. The witnesses press the may know: "that ye may know that ye have eternal life" — that they may know with assurance; the may know; the assurance; the settled; the eternal life; the possess. The expositors draw the lesson on the not doubtful: not a doubtful hope but a settled knowledge; the not doubtful; the settled knowledge; the assurance; the present possession; the sure. The witnesses observe the believe the more: "and that ye may believe on the name of the Son of God" — confirmed and growing in faith; the believe the more; the confirmed; the growing; the in faith; the firmer. The expositors note the the whole aim: the whole epistle given for this assurance; the the whole aim; the assurance; the believe. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that ye may know that ye have eternal life, and the assuring aim. The verse thus records the assuring purpose of the epistle — teaching that John writes that believers may know with settled assurance that they have eternal life.
14:
"And this is the confidence that we have in him, that, if we ask any thing according to his will, he heareth us:" The witnesses note the confidence of believers in prayer according to God's will. Gill notes "the confidence that we have in him" — either in God to whom prayer is made, or in the Son of God through whose blood believers have boldness; they can come with intrepidity and freedom of speech. The witnesses note John declaring that this is the confidence we have in him, that if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us; the boldness in prayer when asking according to God's will — that John adds another privilege flowing from the believer's standing: "and this is the confidence that we have in him, that, if we ask any thing according to his will, he heareth us"; the witnesses note the ground and the condition of this confidence — the believer may come to God in prayer with "confidence" (boldness, freedom, assurance), not as a stranger but as a child, sure of being heard; and the condition that makes prayer sure is that we ask "according to his will" (in agreement with what God has revealed and purposed, in submission to his wisdom) — for such asking God always "heareth"; so that prayer offered in faith and conformed to God's will has the certain promise of his attentive hearing; if we ask any thing according to his will, he heareth us. The witnesses note this is the confidence that we have in him. The witnesses note that, if we ask any thing according to his will, he heareth us.

The witnesses note the believer's confidence in prayer — that asking according to God's will, he hears us; coming with boldness as a child, sure of being heard when prayer is conformed to his will.

So the witnesses make the verse the confidence in prayer according to God's will. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer may come to God with boldness, sure of being heard when he asks according to God's will. The witnesses note the confidence: "this is the confidence that we have in him" — Gill notes boldness and freedom in prayer; the confidence; the boldness; the freedom; the assurance; the in prayer. The expositors observe the as a child: coming not as a stranger but as a child; the as a child; the not a stranger; the boldly; the freely; the to God. The witnesses press the according to his will: "if we ask any thing according to his will" — the condition of sure prayer; the according to his will; the the condition; the God's will; the revealed; the submission. The expositors draw the lesson on the he heareth us: "he heareth us" — God always hears such asking; the he heareth us; the always hears; the attentive; the the promise; the heard. The witnesses observe the sure of being heard: the believer is sure of being heard; the sure of being heard; the assured; the heard; the certain; the the promise. The expositors note the conformed to his will: prayer conformed to God's will has the certain promise; the conformed to his will; the God's will; the the promise; the certain; the heard. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this is the confidence that we have in him, the that if we ask any thing according to his will he heareth us, and the confidence in prayer. The verse thus records the confidence in prayer according to God's will — teaching that the believer may come with boldness, sure of being heard when he asks according to God's will.
15:
"And if we know that he hear us, whatsoever we ask, we know that we have the petitions that we desired of him." The witnesses note the assurance of receiving what we ask in God's will. Gill notes if we may be assured he does hear and answer all such persons that ask according to his will; Barnes notes that, even though we may not see immediately that the prayer is answered, we may have the utmost confidence that it will be. The witnesses note John reasoning that if we know he hears us, whatsoever we ask, we know we have the petitions we desired of him; the certainty of answered prayer that follows from God's hearing — that John draws the comforting inference from the confidence of v.14: "and if we know that he hear us, whatsoever we ask" (since we are assured that God hears prayer offered according to his will) "we know that we have the petitions that we desired of him"; the witnesses note the chain of assurance — to know that God hears is to know that we have; for the God who hears the prayer of faith will surely grant it, so that the believer may reckon the thing asked as already his own, even before he sees it given; though the answer may be delayed or come in God's own way, the praying believer has the petitions as good as in hand, resting on the faithfulness of the God who hears; we know that we have the petitions that we desired of him. The witnesses note if we know that he hear us, whatsoever we ask. The witnesses note we know that we have the petitions that we desired of him.

The witnesses note the certainty of answered prayer — to know God hears is to know we have; the believer reckoning the thing asked as already his own, resting on the faithfulness of the God who hears.

So the witnesses make the verse the assurance of receiving what we ask. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since God hears prayer in his will, the believer knows he has the petitions, as good as in hand. The witnesses note the know he hears: "if we know that he hear us, whatsoever we ask" — Gill notes assured God hears such prayer; the know he hears; the assured; the hears; the such prayer; the in his will. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes even if not seen at once, utmost confidence it will be; the not seen at once; the confidence; the will be; the delayed; the assured. The witnesses press the we have the petitions: "we know that we have the petitions" — we know we have what we asked; the we have the petitions; the we have; the what asked; the know; the granted. The expositors draw the lesson on the to know he hears is to know we have: to know God hears is to know we have; the to know he hears; the to know we have; the the chain; the assurance; the granted. The witnesses observe the as good as in hand: the thing asked is as good as in hand; the as good as in hand; the already his own; the reckons; the before given; the sure. The expositors note the the faithful God: resting on the faithfulness of the God who hears; the the faithful God; the faithfulness; the the God who hears; the resting; the sure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if we know that he hear us whatsoever we ask, the we know that we have the petitions that we desired of him, and the certainty of answered prayer. The verse thus records the assurance of receiving what we ask — teaching that, since God hears, the believer knows he has the petitions, as good as in hand.
16:
"If any man see his brother sin a sin which is not unto death, he shall ask, and he shall give him life for them that sin not unto death. There is a sin unto death: I do not say that he shall pray for it." The witnesses note the prayer for the sinning brother, with the exception of the sin unto death. Gill notes those who have such an interest at the throne of grace should make use of it for others, particularly for fallen believers — a "brother" in a spiritual sense, judged to be born again. The witnesses note John charging that if any see his brother sin a sin not unto death, he shall ask and God shall give him life, but there is a sin unto death for which he does not bid him pray; the intercession for the erring brother, and the much-debated sin unto death — that John applies the confidence in prayer (vv.14-15) to intercession for others: "if any man see his brother sin a sin which is not unto death, he shall ask, and he shall give him life for them that sin not unto death"; the witnesses note that the believer, seeing a brother fall into sin "not unto death" (a sin not of the deadliest, fatal kind), is to pray for him, and God will grant the erring one recovery and life; but John adds a solemn exception: "there is a sin unto death: I do not say that he shall pray for it"; the witnesses note the great debate over the "sin unto death" — some take it of a sin punished with bodily death, others of apostasy or the unpardonable sin against the Holy Ghost, others of a course of sin that ends in death — but all agree John bids the believer pray for the recoverable, while not commanding prayer for the one whose sin is unto death; he shall ask, and he shall give him life. The witnesses note if any man see his brother sin a sin which is not unto death, he shall ask, and he shall give him life. The witnesses note there is a sin unto death: I do not say that he shall pray for it.

The witnesses note the intercession for the sinning brother — praying for one whose sin is not unto death, that God give him life — with the solemn, much-debated exception of the sin unto death.

So the witnesses make the verse the prayer for the erring brother. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer prays for a brother's recoverable sin, while John does not command prayer for the sin unto death. The witnesses note the see his brother sin: "if any man see his brother sin a sin which is not unto death" — Gill notes a fallen believer; the see his brother sin; the a fallen believer; the the brother; the spiritual; the sins. The witnesses present the debate: the witnesses note the great debate on the "sin unto death" — some of a sin punished with bodily death, others of apostasy or the unpardonable sin, others of a course of sin ending in death. The expositors draw the lesson on the I do not say: "there is a sin unto death: I do not say that he shall pray for it" — not commanding prayer for it; the I do not say; the not commanding; the the exception. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the there is a sin unto death I do not say that he shall pray for it, and the intercession. The verse thus records the prayer for the erring brother — teaching that the believer prays for a brother's recoverable sin, while John does not command prayer for the sin unto death.
17:
"All unrighteousness is sin: and there is a sin not unto death." The witnesses note that all unrighteousness is sin, yet not all sin is unto death. Gill notes all unrighteousness against God or man is a sin against the law of God, and the wrath of God is revealed against it, and it deserves death; yet all unrighteousness is not unto death, as the sins of David, which were put away. The witnesses note John declaring that all unrighteousness is sin, and there is a sin not unto death; the distinction between sin in general and the sin not unto death — that John, lest any think some unrighteousness is no sin, lays down the general truth and the needful distinction: "all unrighteousness is sin" (every act of unrighteousness, against God or man, is truly sin, a transgression of God's law deserving his wrath) — "and there is a sin not unto death"; the witnesses note the balance — on the one hand, John does not make light of any sin, for all unrighteousness is sin and in its nature deserves death; yet, on the other, he distinguishes, "there is a sin not unto death," sins into which even true believers fall (as David's) which, through grace and the blood of Christ, are forgiven and do not bring final ruin; so that all sin is real and grievous, but not all sin is the deadly, unrecoverable sin; all unrighteousness is sin: and there is a sin not unto death. The witnesses note all unrighteousness is sin. The witnesses note and there is a sin not unto death.

The witnesses note that all unrighteousness is sin, yet not all is unto death — John making light of no sin, yet distinguishing the sins believers fall into, forgiven through grace, from the deadly sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the distinction between sin and the sin unto death. The lesson the expositors draw is that all unrighteousness is truly sin deserving death, yet not all is the deadly, unrecoverable sin. The witnesses note the all unrighteousness: "all unrighteousness is sin" — Gill notes every unrighteousness is sin against God's law; the all unrighteousness; the every act; the against God's law; the deserves death; the sin. The expositors observe the deserves wrath: in its nature it deserves God's wrath; the deserves wrath; the God's wrath; the deserves death; the the law; the sin. The witnesses press the not unto death: "and there is a sin not unto death" — yet not all sin is unto death; the not unto death; the not all; the not deadly; the recoverable; the the distinction. The expositors draw the lesson on the David's sins: as David's sins, put away through grace; the David's sins; the put away; the through grace; the forgiven; the recovered. The witnesses observe the believers fall: sins into which true believers fall; the believers fall; the true believers; the fall; the forgiven; the the blood. The expositors note the all real yet not all deadly: all sin is real and grievous, but not all is the deadly sin; the all real yet not all deadly; the grievous; the not all deadly; the the distinction; the recoverable. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the all unrighteousness is sin, the and there is a sin not unto death, and the distinction. The verse thus records the distinction between sin and the sin unto death — teaching that all unrighteousness is truly sin deserving death, yet not all is the deadly sin.
18:
"We know that whosoever is born of God sinneth not; but he that is begotten of God keepeth himself, and that wicked one toucheth him not." The witnesses note the preservation of the regenerate from the dominion of sin and the touch of Satan. Gill notes whosoever is born of God, regenerated by his Spirit and grace, made a partaker of the divine nature and having every grace implanted, sinneth not. Matthew Henry notes a recapitulation of the privileges of sound believers. The witnesses note John declaring that whosoever is born of God sinneth not, but he that is begotten of God keeps himself, and the wicked one touches him not; the kept and guarded life of the regenerate, not under sin's dominion nor Satan's grip — that John affirms a great privilege of the new birth: "we know that whosoever is born of God sinneth not"; the witnesses note the famous tension with John's earlier word that he who says he has no sin deceives himself (1:8) — the meaning is not that the born-again never commit any sin at all, but that the regenerate does not sin as the dominant course of his life, does not live in sin or practise it habitually, for the principle of grace in him resists it — "but he that is begotten of God keepeth himself" (he watches and guards himself, kept also by God) "and that wicked one toucheth him not" (Satan cannot fatally grasp or destroy him); so that the child of God is preserved from the reign of sin and the deadly touch of the evil one; whosoever is born of God sinneth not. The witnesses note we know that whosoever is born of God sinneth not. The witnesses note he that is begotten of God keepeth himself, and that wicked one toucheth him not.

The witnesses note the kept life of the regenerate — not sinning as the course of his life, keeping himself, untouched fatally by the wicked one; preserved from the reign of sin and Satan's deadly grip.

So the witnesses make the verse the preservation of the regenerate. The lesson the expositors draw is that the born-again does not live in sin but keeps himself, and the wicked one cannot fatally touch him. The witnesses note the born of God sinneth not: "whosoever is born of God sinneth not" — Gill notes the regenerate does not sin; the born of God sinneth not; the the regenerate; the sinneth not; the the new birth; the grace. The expositors present the tension: the witnesses note the famous tension with 1:8 — the meaning is not that the believer never sins at all, but that he does not sin as the dominant course of life, does not live in or practise sin habitually. The witnesses press the keepeth himself: "but he that is begotten of God keepeth himself" — he watches and guards himself, kept by God; the keepeth himself; the watches; the guards; the kept by God; the preserved. The expositors draw the lesson on the the wicked one: "that wicked one toucheth him not" — Satan cannot fatally grasp him; the the wicked one; the Satan; the cannot grasp; the not fatally; the destroy. The witnesses observe the not under sin's dominion: not under sin's dominion nor Satan's grip; the not under sin's dominion; the not Satan's grip; the preserved; the the reign; the kept. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that is begotten of God keepeth himself and that wicked one toucheth him not, and the kept life. The verse thus records the preservation of the regenerate — teaching that the born-again does not live in sin but keeps himself, and the wicked one cannot fatally touch him.
19:
"And we know that we are of God, and the whole world lieth in wickedness." The witnesses note the believer's knowledge of his standing in God, against the world in wickedness. Gill notes "we know that we are of God," the sons of God and regenerated by him; this is known by the Spirit of God witnessing to their spirits, and by the fruits of regenerating grace, as love to the brethren. The witnesses note John declaring that we know we are of God, and the whole world lies in wickedness; the believer's assured standing in God set against the whole world lying in evil — that John sets the believer's blessed certainty against the dark state of the world: "and we know that we are of God" (believers know, by the Spirit's witness and the fruits of grace, that they belong to God, are his children, on his side) — "and the whole world lieth in wickedness"; the witnesses note the sharp contrast — over against the people of God stands "the whole world," not a part but the whole mass of the unregenerate, which "lieth in wickedness" (lies sunk and steeped in evil, and, as some render, lies "in the wicked one," under the power of Satan); so that there are but two great companies — those who are "of God," and the whole world lying in the grip of evil, and the believer knows to which he belongs; the whole world lieth in wickedness. The witnesses note we know that we are of God. The witnesses note and the whole world lieth in wickedness.

The witnesses note the believer's assured standing in God set against the world in evil — believers knowing they are of God, while the whole world lies sunk in wickedness, under Satan's power.

So the witnesses make the verse the believer's standing against the world's wickedness. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers know they are of God, while the whole world lies steeped in evil. The witnesses note the we are of God: "we know that we are of God" — Gill notes known by the Spirit's witness and fruits; the we are of God; the the Spirit's witness; the the fruits; the his children; the on his side. The expositors observe the the fruits: known by the fruits of grace, as love to the brethren; the the fruits; the of grace; the love; the the brethren; the regenerating. The witnesses press the the whole world: "and the whole world lieth in wickedness" — not a part but the whole mass; the the whole world; the the whole mass; the the unregenerate; the not a part; the all. The expositors draw the lesson on the lieth in wickedness: "lieth in wickedness" — sunk and steeped in evil; the lieth in wickedness; the sunk; the steeped; the in evil; the under Satan. The witnesses observe the in the wicked one: as some render, lies "in the wicked one," under Satan's power; the in the wicked one; the under Satan; the his power; the the grip; the evil. The expositors note the two companies: there are but two companies, those of God and the world; the two companies; the of God; the the world; the the contrast; the belongs. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we know that we are of God, the and the whole world lieth in wickedness, and the believer's standing. The verse thus records the believer's standing against the world's wickedness — teaching that believers know they are of God, while the whole world lies steeped in evil.
20:
"And we know that the Son of God is come, and hath given us an understanding, that we may know him that is true, and we are in him that is true, even in his Son Jesus Christ. This is the true God, and eternal life." The witnesses note the coming of the Son, the gift of understanding, and the deity of Christ. Gill notes the Son of God is come, the second Person, equal to the Father and of the same nature, come from heaven by assumption of nature, become incarnate to work out salvation. The witnesses note John declaring that the Son of God is come and has given us understanding to know him that is true, and we are in him that is true, even in his Son Jesus Christ; this is the true God and eternal life; the incarnate Son giving us to know and be in the true God, who is Christ himself — that John gathers up the great certainties: "we know that the Son of God is come" (truly incarnate) "and hath given us an understanding, that we may know him that is true" (a spiritual understanding to know the true God) "and we are in him that is true, even in his Son Jesus Christ"; and he crowns it: "this is the true God, and eternal life"; the witnesses note the weighty witness to Christ's deity — the most natural reading takes "this is the true God, and eternal life" of the nearest antecedent, "his Son Jesus Christ," so that Christ himself is here expressly called "the true God" and "eternal life," a clear testimony to his full divinity; so that the believer, brought by the incarnate Son to know and be in the true God, finds that Son to be himself the true God and the life eternal; this is the true God, and eternal life. The witnesses note the Son of God is come, and hath given us an understanding, that we may know him that is true. The witnesses note this is the true God, and eternal life.

The witnesses note the incarnate Son giving understanding to know the true God — and the weighty witness that "this is the true God, and eternal life" names Christ himself, a clear testimony to his deity.

So the witnesses make the verse the Son giving knowledge of the true God and Christ's deity. The lesson the expositors draw is that the incarnate Son gives us to know and be in the true God, who is Christ himself, the true God and eternal life. The witnesses note the Son is come: "the Son of God is come" — Gill notes truly incarnate, from heaven; the Son is come; the incarnate; the from heaven; the equal to the Father; the salvation. The expositors observe the given understanding: "hath given us an understanding, that we may know him that is true" — a spiritual understanding to know God; the given understanding; the spiritual; the the true God; the knowledge. The witnesses press the we are in him: "and we are in him that is true, even in his Son Jesus Christ" — united to the true God in Christ; the we are in him; the in Christ; the united; the his Son. The expositors draw the lesson on the the true God: "this is the true God, and eternal life" — Christ called the true God; the the true God; the eternal life; the Christ; the named; the deity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this is the true God and eternal life, and the deity of Christ. The verse thus records the Son giving knowledge of the true God and Christ's deity — teaching that the incarnate Son gives us to know and be in the true God, who is Christ himself, the true God and eternal life.
21:
"Little children, keep yourselves from idols. Amen." The witnesses note the closing charge to keep from idols. Gill notes "keep yourselves from idols" — from heathen idols and idolatry, into which the saints might be drawn by dwelling among heathen idolaters, being related to them, and the freedom used in eating things sacrificed to idols. The witnesses note John closing the epistle with the charge, "Little children, keep yourselves from idols. Amen"; the tender final warning to guard against all idolatry — that John ends his letter with a short, affectionate, and pointed exhortation: "little children" (the tender name he loves to use) "keep yourselves from idols. Amen"; the witnesses note the fitness of this close — having shown them the true God, who is found in his Son Jesus Christ (v.20), John warns them to keep clear of all false gods; the believers, living among heathen idolaters, were in danger of being drawn into idolatry by kinship and custom, and so are charged to guard themselves; the witnesses note that "idols" may reach beyond carved images to whatever a man sets in God's place in his heart, so that the warning is to keep the heart for the true God alone; keep yourselves from idols. Amen. The witnesses note little children, keep yourselves from idols. The witnesses note Amen.

The witnesses note the tender final charge — "keep yourselves from idols" — warning the believers among heathen idolaters to guard against all idolatry, keeping the heart for the true God alone.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing charge against idolatry. The lesson the expositors draw is that John, having shown the true God, charges the believers to keep clear of all idols. The witnesses note the little children: "little children" — Gill notes the tender name John loves; the little children; the tender name; the affectionate; the loves; the close. The expositors observe the keep from idols: "keep yourselves from idols" — from heathen idols and idolatry; the keep from idols; the heathen idols; the idolatry; the guard; the clear. The witnesses press the among idolaters: the believers lived among heathen idolaters; the among idolaters; the heathen; the dwelling; the related; the the custom. The expositors draw the lesson on the the danger: in danger of being drawn in by kinship and custom; the the danger; the drawn in; the kinship; the custom; the sacrificed. The witnesses observe the having shown the true God: having shown them the true God, he warns of false gods; the having shown the true God; the the true God; the false gods; the warns; the clear. The expositors note the the heart: "idols" reach to whatever a man sets in God's place; the the heart; the whatever; the in God's place; the false god; the the heart. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the little children keep yourselves from idols, the Amen, and the charge against idolatry. The verse thus records the closing charge against idolatry — teaching that John, having shown the true God, charges the believers to keep clear of all idols.
